title: Allan govt unveils $14m rescue package as it braces for AI-driven job losses
summary: The Allan government has announced a $14 million package to help Victorian workers facing job losses due to AI. The plan includes an $8.2 million career rescue scheme and a new TAFE centre to support those in at-risk industries.
ticker_line: Victoria launches $14m AI job rescue package
key_points: The AI Investment Package aims to support workers in danger of losing jobs.
Barney Glover warned job losses could exceed 600,000 nationwide.
The new TAFE Centre of Excellence will open at Chisholm Institute in Frankston.
Treasury officials admit they lack data on AI's financial impact.
The government will not use AI to reduce public service jobs.
author: Alex White
heading: Data Science and AI

title: NAB fixed interest rate hike signals more pain to come for borrowers
summary: National Australia Bank (NAB) has raised its fixed mortgage rates by 15 basis points, indicating potential further interest rate hikes ahead. The lowest fixed rate is now 6.49%, and forecasts suggest rates could rise again by August as the Reserve Bank of Australia continues to combat inflation.
ticker_line: NAB raises fixed mortgage rates amid inflation fight
key_points: NAB is the first major bank to increase fixed rates after May's cash rate hike.
Fixed rates for one and two-year mortgages are now 6.49%.
NAB expects interest rates to rise by another 25 basis points by August.
The Reserve Bank of Australia has raised the cash rate to 4.35%.
Only three lenders now offer fixed rates below 6%.

heading: Banking and Economy

title: Super not an answer to all your tax issues
summary: Recent budget changes have made superannuation a more tax-friendly investment, but it may not be suitable for everyone due to strict withdrawal rules and new taxes on high balances. Many Australians still face financial pressures that make super less appealing for younger age groups.
ticker_line: Superannuation gains but still not for all
key_points: Superannuation tax rates are lower than other investments.
Withdrawals from super are generally not allowed until age 60.
New taxes on super balances over $3 million start July 1.
Average super balance for those aged 60-64 is around $355,000.
Concerns exist about future changes to superannuation rules.

summary: Over 25,000 property owners in Logan have been informed that council flood maps over-estimated water levels by 1 metre. This finding, from an independent review, has raised concerns about property values and insurance costs.

key_points: Flawed flood maps affected 25,000 Logan properties.
Independent review found flood levels over-estimated by 1m.
Logan City Council plans to remove controversial flood mapping.
Concerns about insurance and property values persist among residents.
Next council meeting scheduled for June 4.

summary: AI data centers now make up 17% of all private investment in Australia, highlighting the growing importance of artificial intelligence in the country's economy. This shift reflects a significant increase in funding and interest in AI technologies.

Reflects growing importance of AI in Australia
Indicates increased funding and interest in AI technologies

title: The data centre mega-hub being quietly mapped out in Melbourne’s west
summary: A large data centre hub, called the Victorian AI Hub, is planned for Plumpton, Melbourne, covering 350 hectares and aiming for 2.4 gigawatts of power capacity. key_points: The hub will be the largest data centre in Australia.

It will use reclaimed water for cooling systems.
The Victorian government is considering the proposal, which may face planning hurdles.

heading: Data Science and AI

title: Macquarie could disrupt big four's 'lazy' $370b edge
summary: Macquarie is challenging Australia's big four banks by offering higher interest rates on savings, which could significantly impact the banks' profits. Analysts warn that if the major banks respond by raising rates, their earnings could drop by up to 40%.

summary: The federal budget predicts $13.7 billion more investment in new housing than the Reserve Bank, suggesting the government may have overestimated housing supply by over 10,000 homes. This discrepancy raises concerns about meeting Labor's goal of 1.2 million new homes.

key_points: Treasury forecasts $315 billion in housing investment for 2026-27 and 2027-28.
RBA forecasts $301 billion for the same period.
Government changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax expected to reduce housing supply by 35,000 homes over the next decade.

heading: Banking and Economy

key_points: HESTA is the major investor in Synthesis Capital.
Synthesis Capital has raised nearly $60 million for its first fund.
The firm targets early-stage health and medical tech start-ups.
Synthesis Capital is led by experienced professionals from the medtech sector.
The fund aims to help Australian scientists access global investment.

title: Social impact investors 'can fix NDIS'
summary: Michael Traill proposes a major change to the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) by consolidating its 270,000 providers into a few large operators backed by social impact investors to improve efficiency and save costs. This plan aims to address the scheme's financial issues and achieve $185 billion in budget savings over the next decade.
ticker_line: Traill's bold plan to reform NDIS funding
key_points: Traill's plan involves reducing providers to a few large operators.
Social impact investing would fund the new structure.
The NDIS currently has 250,000 unregistered and 17,000 registered providers.

summary: A report shows the economic gap between younger and older Australians is growing, with younger people struggling more with home ownership and stagnant wages. This inequality could reach record levels in the coming years.

key_points: Younger Australians face rising costs and stagnant wages.
Home ownership for under-35s is at 1947 levels.
It now takes an average of 11 years to save for a 20% deposit.
Older generations have benefited from rising house prices.
79% of Australians believe children will be worse off financially.

key_points: Bendigo Bank to transfer tech workers to Infosys.
Unions claim new contracts are worse than expected.
Contracts allow Infosys to set work hours and locations.
Pay could decrease during crises with no guaranteed raises.
FSU advises members against signing the contracts.
Bendigo Bank aims to improve IT service delivery.

title: Rate hold expectations on slowdown
summary: Inflation in Australia slowed to 4.2% in April, partly due to the government's fuel excise cut, leading economists to expect the Reserve Bank to keep interest rates steady in June. However, rising costs in housing and transport, along with ongoing global issues, may continue to challenge inflation.

key_points: Inflation dropped from 4.6% in March to 4.2% in April.
Fuel prices fell 7% after the fuel excise was cut to 26.3¢.
The fuel excise cut is expected to cost the government over $2.5 billion.
Housing costs rose 6.3% and transport costs rose 6.6% in April.
Economists predict the Reserve Bank will hold rates at 4.35% in June.

summary: Westpac has been fined $26 million by the Federal Court for not properly handling over 200 customer requests for financial help between 2017 and 2023. The court found the bank was grossly negligent in its response to customers facing hardships like job loss and illness.

key_points: Westpac fined $26 million for negligence.

summary: Australia's economy is slowing down while inflation stays high, creating a tough situation for the Reserve Bank. With rising unemployment and concerns over new tax changes, the bank may need to decide soon whether to raise interest rates or keep them steady.

key_points: Core inflation rose to 3.4%, above the RBA's target.
Consumer sentiment dropped 20% since late 2025.
Unemployment reached 4.5%, the highest since November 2021.
Government spending is at 27% of GDP, the highest in 30 years.
Retail stocks have significantly declined, with some down over 70%.
Concerns over new tax changes affecting investor confidence.

key_points: Annual inflation fell to 4.2% in April 2026.
The trimmed mean inflation measure rose to 3.4%.
Economists believe the Reserve Bank may hold rates steady at 4.35%.
Unemployment increased to 4.5% recently.
Future rate hikes could still occur depending on global energy prices.

summary: An audit has revealed that the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) needs to fix important cybersecurity issues before the upcoming census on August 11, 2026. key_points: ABS plans to conduct the census online, expecting 85% participation.
The audit found unresolved cybersecurity vulnerabilities.
Concerns were raised about the ABS's risk assessment and governance.
The 2016 census faced major issues due to cyberattacks.
ABS claims it is continuously reassessing and addressing cyber threats.

summary: IT literacy among 15 to 16-year-olds in NSW has dropped to record lows, with only 37% achieving a proficient standard in 2025, down from 44% in 2022. This decline reflects a national trend, despite many students reporting regular use of technology and AI.

key_points: Only 37% of NSW year 10 students are proficient in IT.
This is a significant drop from 44% in 2022 and 67% in 2008.
NSW ranks mid-range nationally, lower than ACT and slightly below Victoria and WA.
Year 6 IT literacy remains steady, just above the national average.
AI use is increasing among students, with many using it for schoolwork.

summary: A survey shows that nearly 80% of workers using AI have not received any formal training from their employers, raising concerns about companies' readiness to adapt to new technologies. While many employees recognise the need for AI skills, employers are struggling to provide the necessary training and support.

key_points: 80% of workers using AI lack formal training.
Only 22% of professionals are concerned about AI adaptation.

key_points: House prices may drop by 5% according to Macquarie.
Tax changes could lead to 20 years of stagnant prices.
Investor activity in established housing could fall by over 50%.

summary: The Insurance Council of Australia is urging for major changes to the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR) to ensure its long-term sustainability, rather than just finding new funding methods. They believe reforms should focus on the root causes of costs and ensure that only those responsible for misconduct fund the compensation.
ticker_line: Insurance Council pushes for CSLR reform
key_points: ICA calls for structural reform of CSLR.

ticker_line: Coalition's support for NDIS cuts uncertain
key_points: Labor aims to reduce NDIS growth from 11% to 4.5%.

title: Australians back tougher crackdown on super switching advertisements
summary: A new survey shows that 80% of Australians want the government to ban misleading superannuation ads that encourage risky fund switches. The findings highlight the need for stronger protections after 12,000 people lost $1.2 billion due to the collapse of certain super funds.

key_points: 80% of Australians support banning predatory super ads.
45% have seen misleading super ads on social media.
12,000 people lost $1.2 billion from collapsed funds.
Over 70% struggle to tell scams from legitimate ads.
Calls for stronger oversight and accountability in the super industry.
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation and Investments

title: Funds urged to boost insurance engagement among younger members
summary: A study by TAL shows that younger Australians and women often lack understanding of their insurance in superannuation. It suggests super funds should improve communication and guidance to help members feel more secure about their coverage.
ticker_line: Funds urged to engage younger members on insurance
key_points: 77% of members who adjusted their insurance feel protected.
64% of members with known cover feel adequately protected.
55% believe super funds should provide insurance guidance.
80% want annual insurance updates from their super fund.
74% of 25-34 year-olds unaware of default insurance in super.
71% of uninsured women never looked into life insurance.
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation and Investments

title: Aware targets adviser demand with expanded technical education support
summary: Aware Super is boosting support for financial advisers by hiring Troy Smith as senior manager of technical services. He will lead education initiatives to help advisers with complex retirement planning and superannuation strategies.
ticker_line: Aware Super enhances adviser support with new hire
key_points: Troy Smith appointed as senior manager, technical services.
Focus on technical education for financial advisers.
Smith has experience from Insignia Financial and ANZ.
Role aims to enhance adviser support and client guidance.
Aware Super sees growing adviser engagement and partnerships.
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation and Investments

title: Digital ID program gets $650M for expansion in Australian federal budget
summary: The Australian government is investing $654.3 million in its 2026-27 budget to expand the Digital ID program, aiming to simplify identity verification for over 255 government services. This funding will enhance the Australian Government Digital ID System and improve data sharing and security measures.
ticker_line: Australia boosts Digital ID program with $654M investment
key_points: Investment of $654.3 million for Digital ID expansion.
Supports a 'tell-us-once' approach for accessing services.
Includes $135.2 million for Services Australia to manage the Digital ID Exchange.
$357.4 million allocated to the Australian Taxation Office for security updates.
$62 million invested in the Consumer Data Right program.

heading: Digital Economy

title: High Street's restructure bets against the agency roll-up model
summary: High Street Underwriting Agency's new parent company, 11th Avenue, is taking a different approach to agency consolidation by keeping each agency's independence and decision-making intact, rather than merging them into a larger corporate structure.
ticker_line: High Street bets on agency independence amid consolidation
key_points: Australia's underwriting agency sector is growing rapidly, with over 300 agencies writing more than $10 billion in premiums.
Brokers value technical expertise and responsiveness from agencies, which may be lost in larger corporate structures.
11th Avenue aims to maintain the unique culture and decision-making of its agencies while providing shared technology and support.
APRA is concerned about the risks of outsourced underwriting and is urging better governance in agencies.

heading: General Insurance

title: A win delivered for super members
summary: The Australian government will keep current tax rules for superannuation, including SMSFs, which means no changes to capital gains tax for super funds. This decision is seen as a positive outcome for the 19 million Australians with super accounts, ensuring stability in their retirement savings.
ticker_line: Government maintains stable tax rules for superannuation
key_points: Government excludes superannuation from capital gains tax changes.
ASFA welcomes the decision, calling it a win for super members.
Current CGT settings for super remain unchanged until July 2027.
Superannuation offers lower tax rates compared to personal investments.
Super's $4.5 trillion savings pool supports Australian businesses.

heading: Superannuation and Investments

summary: The Super Members Council has welcomed a $17.8 million funding boost for ASIC in the 2026 federal budget to enhance investor protection and strengthen the superannuation system.
key_points: The funding will be allocated over four years, starting from the 2026/27 income year.
$10.3 million will be used in the next financial year to improve ASIC's data supervision capabilities.
$7.6 million will strengthen governance for managed investment schemes and support ongoing consumer protection reforms.

heading: Superannuation and Investments

title: Australian ETF market surges to record $346bn

International equities attracted $2.6 billion, while Australian equities drew $1.5 billion in inflows.
Passive investment strategies accounted for over $5 billion of April inflows, with Vanguard, Betashares, and iShares being the largest issuers.

heading: Banking and Economy

summary: The Actuaries Institute and the Australian Banking Association have praised the government's Federal Budget measures aimed at enhancing productivity in the Australian economy, highlighting the importance of ongoing reforms for sustainable growth.

Projected long-run GDP growth is estimated at $13 billion per year.
Reforms include making the instant asset write-off permanent to encourage business investment.
author: Riddhima Talwani
heading: Actuaries

summary: The 2026-27 Federal Budget includes measures affecting the insurance sector, such as a $3.4 million allocation for property insurance reforms, a doubled APRA approval threshold, and notable omissions regarding the Hazards Insurance Partnership.
key_points: A $3.4 million allocation targets property insurance costs and aims to standardise natural hazard definitions.
APRA's approval threshold for insurers doubles from $5 billion to $10 billion, potentially fostering competition.

heading: General Insurance

key_points: The time between an attacker gaining access and deploying ransomware has decreased by about 70% since 2021.
Australia is now among the top 10 most targeted countries for cyber attacks, with a rise in pre-encryption data theft and human manipulation tactics.
The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner reported that 59% of data breach notifications were due to malicious activity, with human error contributing to over a third of breaches.
author: Roxanne Libatique
heading: Data Science and AI

title: Allianz storms into 2026 as P&C arm powers blockbuster quarter

key_points: Operating profit increased by 6.6% year-on-year to €4.5 billion.
Core net income rose 48.4% to €3.8 billion, influenced by one-off gains.
Total business volume decreased to €53.0 billion, but internal growth remained at 3.5%.

heading: General Insurance

heading: Actuaries

heading: Data Science and AI

$1 billion dedicated to improving personal care services in the Support at Home program

key_points: AI can significantly reduce the time for control testing in risk management.
Organisations should evaluate their risk management structures with a fresh perspective, rather than retrofitting new tools into existing models.
Effective governance and a tailored AI strategy are essential for maximising the benefits of AI in risk management.
author: Isobel Markham
heading: Risk Management

title: Tech boss backs Aussie AI amid tax fears
summary: Amazon Web Services' new Australian managing director, Chris Casey, expresses confidence in local companies becoming AI leaders despite concerns over tax reforms, while highlighting the need for skills development and announcing a $20bn investment in AI infrastructure.
key_points: AWS plans to invest $20bn in Australian AI infrastructure over the next five years.
Concerns arise from Treasurer Jim Chalmers' capital gains tax reforms, which may hinder investment in startups.
AWS has trained 400,000 Australians in AI skills and is launching new renewable energy projects to meet rising power demands.
author: Jared Lynch
heading: Data Science and AI

title: Massive problem budget failed to fix
summary: Australia's gross debt is set to exceed $1 trillion despite significant budget reforms aimed at curbing negative gearing and capital gains tax, with projected debt rising to $1.249 trillion by 2028.
key_points: Treasurer Jim Chalmers announced major tax reforms including the removal of the 50% capital gains tax discount and restrictions on negative gearing.
Gross national debt is expected to rise from $928.6 billion in 2024-2025 to $1.051 trillion by 2027.
The budget reforms aim to improve housing affordability but face criticism for potentially reducing housing supply.
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Actuaries

title: Surfside residents fear homes could be lost to sea in next storm
summary: Residents of Surfside Beach, NSW, are worried their homes could be lost to the sea due to erosion and lack of effective government action. Despite previous emergency measures, many feel urgent solutions are needed as storms continue to threaten their properties.
ticker_line: Surfside residents fear homes lost to erosion
key_points: Residents fear homes could fall into the ocean.
Jason and Ange Ford highlight lack of insurance for tidal damage.
Bega MP Michael Holland met with residents but no formal follow-up meeting occurred.
Eurobodalla Shire Council completed temporary dune repairs but erosion remains a concern.
Long-term coastal management plan in place, but residents believe it is ineffective.
heading: General Insurance

title: Chalmers unveils biggest attack on wealth since federation
summary: The 2026 Chalmers budget introduces significant changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing, impacting housing prices and wealth distribution in Australia.
key_points: The budget is described as the largest attack on national wealth since federation, targeting high-value properties and investment assets.
Changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing are expected to lower housing prices and affect bank profits, insurance companies, and local governments.
The budget may force entrepreneurs to relocate to countries with more favourable tax regimes, impacting business growth and investment.
author: Robert Gottliebsen
heading: Banking and Economy

title: Treasury warns of inflation surge, growth slide
summary: Australia's Treasury warns of potential inflation surging to 7.25% and negative GDP growth due to ongoing geopolitical tensions, while a more optimistic scenario predicts inflation halving to 2.5% next year.
key_points: Worst-case scenario predicts inflation at 7.25% and GDP decline of 0.5% amid oil price spikes.
Base case forecasts inflation to drop to 2.5% next year if oil prices ease.
RBA governor warns that government handouts could exacerbate inflation challenges.
author: MATTHEW CRANSTON , THOMAS HENRY
heading: Banking and Economy

title: WA opposition slams state budget as ‘reckless and unsophisticated’
summary: The WA opposition has condemned the 2026-27 state budget as reckless and unsophisticated, arguing it fails to address inflation and economic challenges.
key_points: Opposition claims the budget leads to reckless spending and no reforms.
Treasurer disputes claims that inflation is 'made in WA' and defends budget spending.
The budget has faced widespread criticism from economists and industry groups.
author: Holly Thompson
heading: Banking and Economy

title: Stop training people for yesterday’s jobs: AI demands we redesign work
summary: The shift towards AI as a general-purpose capability necessitates a redesign of work processes before implementing reskilling initiatives to enhance productivity and business value.
key_points: AI is transforming work structures and requires redesign rather than just reskilling.
Effective work design significantly increases the likelihood of surpassing AI return on investment.
Organisations must focus on continuous learning and adapting processes to integrate AI effectively.
author: Elise Sharpley
heading: Data Science and AI

title: ISACA poll finds AI adoption outpaces governance and ROI
summary: A recent ISACA survey reveals that while AI adoption is widespread, many organisations struggle with governance, ROI visibility, and incident response related to AI systems.
key_points: 90% of organisations believe staff are using AI, but only 22% report satisfactory ROI.
56% are unsure about the time required to halt AI systems during security incidents.
38% of organisations have a formal AI policy, indicating a need for improved governance.
heading: Data Science and AI

title: Hackers winning cyber war
summary: Australia's cybersecurity landscape is deteriorating, with a significant rise in severe vulnerabilities identified by ethical hackers, highlighting the increasing sophistication of cyber threats.
key_points: CyberCX's Hack Report reveals over 1800 severe risks uncovered in 2025.
One in three security assessments showed severe findings, indicating a slight improvement but still a major concern.
Education and healthcare sectors are particularly vulnerable, with new AI systems being exploited by hackers.
author: Eilidh Sproul-Mellis
heading: Data Science and AI

title: Jim Chalmers is going for broke. Will we end up a poorer country?
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers is set to unveil a significant budget aimed at tax reform amidst rising inflation and economic challenges, with proposals that may impact intergenerational equity and investment strategies.
key_points: Chalmers plans to cut the capital gains tax discount and scrap negative gearing.
The budget aims to address intergenerational inequity and improve productivity.
Higher inflation is expected to increase government revenues but complicate fiscal management.
author: Matthew Cranston
heading: Actuaries

title: Exclusive: RBA rates Labor bad on homes
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) has criticized Labor's housing policies, saying they have not improved home supply and may have raised prices. Labor's plans to increase housing availability are under scrutiny as migration rates and other factors continue to impact affordability.
ticker_line: RBA criticizes Labor's housing policies and impact
key_points: RBA officials described Labor's housing agenda as 'relatively modest'.
Labor's Help to Buy initiative and 5% deposit scheme were criticized for potentially increasing prices.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers claimed $25 billion could be redirected to new housing.
Labor aims to build 1.2 million new homes by 2030 but is currently falling short.
Migration rates and regulations are seen as key factors affecting housing supply.
author: Paul Sakkal | Chief political corre
heading: Banking and Economy

title: CEOs get AI twins as clones step up
summary: Australian CEOs are using AI to create digital versions of themselves, helping with tasks like writing memos and managing schedules. This trend is changing corporate communications, but experts warn that human skills are still needed for effective messaging.
ticker_line: CEOs embrace AI clones for corporate tasks
key_points: CEOs like Sukhinder Singh Cassidy of Xero are using AI to clone their voices and assist with tasks.
AI technology is impacting corporate communications and investor relations.
Experts believe human touch is still essential in writing and decision-making.
Companies face new challenges from AI, including misinformation and reputation management.
author: Jared Lynch
heading: Data Science and AI

title: Treasury sets out industry’s APRA levies
summary: Treasury plans to collect about $27.2 million from the general insurance industry to support the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) in the next financial year. The overall levy for APRA is estimated at $297.3 million, which includes costs for life insurance as well.
ticker_line: Treasury outlines APRA levies for insurance sector
key_points: General insurance levy is $27.2 million for 2026-27.
Total APRA recovery requirement is $297.3 million.
Life insurance contributes $19.5 million to the levy.
General insurance represents 9.1% of the total levy.
Life insurance represents 6.4% of the total levy.
APRA will focus on claims management in general insurance.
Submissions on the discussion paper are due by June 14.
heading: Regulation

title: Beware knock-on effects of mental health reform: EML
summary: A report from EML warns that changes to mental health support in workers' compensation could lead to more claims in income protection insurance. It calls for better coordination across Australia's support systems to prevent financial vulnerability and improve recovery outcomes.
ticker_line: EML warns of mental health reform impacts
key_points: EML report highlights risks of fragmented mental health support.
Tighter eligibility for psychological injury may increase income protection claims by 19% in Victoria over the next decade.
NSW, Queensland, and Tasmania are reviewing psychological claim entitlements.
Proposes training for claims teams and dedicated case managers for claimants.
Calls for early intervention and standardised processes across schemes.
heading: Life Insurance

title: Ombudsman slams icare over wage adjustment delays
summary: The NSW Ombudsman has criticized icare for delays in processing wage adjustments for workers' compensation, which caused financial issues for employers. The Ombudsman recommends icare apologize, offer flexible payment plans, and improve its processes.
ticker_line: Ombudsman criticizes icare for wage adjustment delays
key_points: Ombudsman investigated icare after six employer complaints.
Delays ranged from 12 months to over three years.
Invoices for adjustments were as high as $579,188.
Ombudsman recommends formal apology and better payment plans.
icare has accepted the recommendations and is making changes.
heading: General Insurance

title: NSW confirms workers’ comp premiums
summary: NSW employers will soon get workers' compensation renewal notices, with icare confirming premium settings. The minimum premium stays at $240, and the cap on premium increases for some employers will drop from 30% to 25%.
ticker_line: NSW confirms workers' comp premium changes
key_points: NSW government froze industry classification rates for 2026-27 and 2027-28.
Individual premiums may vary based on wages, claims, and incentives.
Employers must pay an excess at the start of a claim.
icare has provided a webinar for industry professionals.
heading: General Insurance

title: Perils lifts November storms loss tally
summary: Storms in Queensland and NSW last November caused insured losses of $2.94 billion, according to Swiss data company Perils. The updated figure shows an increase from the previous estimate of $2.66 billion, with most losses coming from Queensland.
ticker_line: November storms cost $2.94 billion in losses
key_points: Insured losses from November storms total $2.94 billion.
Previous estimate was $2.66 billion.
Losses mainly from property and motor claims.
Queensland accounts for 91% of total losses.
Hailstones reached up to 14cm in diameter.
heading: General Insurance

title: Insurers to revisit storm-hit Clifton
summary: Insurers will hold support sessions in Clifton, Queensland on June 8-9 for residents affected by a hailstorm last November. Policyholders can meet with insurer representatives to get help with claims and recovery.
ticker_line: Insurers offer support for storm-hit Clifton residents
key_points: Support sessions on June 8-9 in Clifton
For residents affected by November hailstorm
Insurers will assist with claims and recovery
No bookings required, sessions from 10am to 4pm
heading: General Insurance

title: Industry profit down amid investment slump
summary: The life insurance industry reported a profit of $121 million for the March quarter, a significant drop from $332 million a year earlier, due to lower investment returns and increased claims. Underwriting profits also fell, with individual disability insurance performing best among product classes.
ticker_line: Life insurance profits decline amid investment slump
key_points: Profit down to $121 million from $332 million year-on-year.
Investment returns fell to $282 million from $800 million.
Underwriting profit decreased to $249 million from $425 million.
Incurred claims rose to $3 billion from $2.97 billion.
Individual disability income insurance made $250 million profit.
heading: Life Insurance

title: Housing price slump in Melbourne continues
summary: Melbourne and Sydney are seeing significant drops in housing prices due to high interest rates and new tax changes. In May, Sydney house values fell to $1.58 million, while Melbourne's median house price dropped to $958,000.
ticker_line: Housing prices continue to fall in Melbourne and Sydney
key_points: Sydney house values down 2.1% over three months.
Melbourne house values down 2.3% for the quarter.
Interest rates now at 4.35% as of May.
Perth house values up 25.6% over the past year.
Rental prices increased by 5.9% annually.
author: Shane Wright and Matthew Knott
heading: Banking and Economy

title: Retirement anxiety grows
summary: Many Australians are delaying retirement by up to four years due to rising living costs, with most expecting to work until 66 instead of their desired 62. A new report shows that people now believe they need over $1 million in superannuation for a comfortable retirement.
ticker_line: Australians delay retirement amid rising costs
key_points: Colonial First State report reveals retirement delays.
Most Australians expect to retire at 66 instead of 62.
Over half worry about insufficient retirement funds.
Average retirement savings target exceeds $1 million.
Financial advice significantly boosts retirement preparedness.
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Superannuation and Investments
